schools.summary: Paul anticipates the charge that he is praising himself: does he need, like others, to commend himself anew? — asked ironically, knowing the Corinthians know him; while 'some others' (the false teachers) came with letters of commendation to or from the churches, Paul needs no such credentials with the Corinthians, whom he had himself converted (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): Paul's true commendation is not paper credentials but the Corinthians themselves, his converts — the living proof of his apostleship.

themes: Do we begin again to commend ourselves
need we... epistles of commendation
disclaiming letters of commendation
the converts as credentials

schools.summary: Paul declares the Corinthians themselves are 'our epistle' (our letter of recommendation) — the proof of his ministry, 'written in our hearts' (cherished there with affection), and 'known and read of all men' (open to all as evidence of his work); their conversion his dearest credential (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the changed lives of the Corinthians are a letter that all can read, testifying to the genuineness and power of Paul's apostolic ministry.

themes: Ye are our epistle written in our hearts
known and read of all men
the Corinthians as Paul's living letter
the converts as credential

schools.summary: The Corinthians are 'the epistle of Christ' — Christ the true author of the letter (their conversion), Paul only the minister (the instrument, the pen) by whom it was written; written not with ink but by 'the Spirit of the living God' who inscribed it, by his regenerating work in their hearts (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): unlike the law written on stone tables (Exodus), this new-covenant writing is on 'fleshy tables of the heart' (Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26) — inward, living, and transforming, the mark of the new covenant of the Spirit.

themes: ye are... the epistle of Christ ministered by us
written... with the Spirit of the living God... in fleshy tables of the heart
the epistle of Christ written by the Spirit
the new-covenant writing on the heart

schools.summary: Paul affirms that he has 'such trust' (confidence, assurance) — concerning his ministry and its fruit (the Corinthians) — and this 'through Christ' and 'to God-ward' (directed toward God); his confidence not self-derived but coming through Christ and exercised before God, a humble, God-referred assurance, not boasting (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul, having spoken of the glory of his ministry, immediately ascribes his confidence to Christ and refers it to God, lest he seem to glory in himself.

themes: such trust have we through Christ to God-ward
Paul's confidence through Christ toward God
assurance not self-derived
glorying not in self

schools.summary: Paul explicitly denies any self-sufficiency: he is not adequate 'of himself' even 'to think any thing as of ourselves' — not even his thoughts, much less his ministry, originate in his own power; all his adequacy for the work comes 'of God,' who alone makes him fit (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): a strong testimony to human insufficiency and the necessity of grace — not even a good thought is of ourselves; all is of God, who gives both the will and the power.

themes: not that we are sufficient of ourselves to think any thing as of ourselves
our sufficiency is of God
all sufficiency of God
human insufficiency and the necessity of grace

schools.summary: God has made Paul 'sufficient' as a minister of the 'new covenant' (the gospel, vs the old Mosaic covenant); 'letter' means the law / the old written covenant (the bare commandment, which condemns and so 'kills'), and 'spirit' the gospel / new covenant ministered by the Spirit (which gives life). Barnes and Ellicott explicitly reject Origen's allegorical reading (letter = the literal sense, spirit = the allegorical/spiritual meaning) — the 'letter' is the whole written code of Judaism, whose work is to make men conscious of sin (Romans 7); the Spirit quickens.

Calvin: by 'letter' Paul means outward preaching that does not reach the heart, by 'spirit' living doctrine working effectually by the Spirit's grace (Jeremiah 31:31); the law is in itself a dead, killing preaching, the gospel life-giving — said in respect of the contrast between the two. He sharply rejects Origen's reading (letter = grammatical sense, spirit = allegory) as a spurious signification that bred the pernicious error of turning Scripture into allegories.

Clarke: Paul does not mean the Old Testament kills and the New gives life, but that the New gives the proper (spiritual) meaning of the Old; both covenants have a letter and a spirit. The Jews rested in the letter, which killed by condemning; resting in the bare letter of the gospel (the outward symbols, the bare 'follow me') likewise profits nothing — the spirit, the inward meaning that leads to Christ, gives life.

debates.question: Do 'letter' and 'spirit' mean the law and the gospel, or the literal and the allegorical/spiritual sense of Scripture?

sides.position: The law / old written covenant (which condemns) and the gospel / new covenant of the Spirit (which gives life)
Resting in the bare external letter (of either covenant) vs the inward spiritual meaning that leads to Christ
The literal grammatical sense vs the allegorical/spiritual sense (Origen) — explicitly rejected as a spurious error
holders: Origen (noted and rejected by Calvin, Barnes)

themes: able ministers of the new testament; not of the letter, but of the spirit
the letter killeth, but the spirit giveth life
the ministry of the new covenant
the letter that kills crux

schools.summary: Paul calls the law (the ten commandments, 'written and engraven in stones,' Exodus 31:18) 'the ministration of death' — because, condemning sin, it brought death, not life; yet even it had a real 'glory,' so that Moses's face shone after Sinai (Exodus 34:29-30) and Israel could not gaze steadily upon it; but that glory 'was to be done away,' fading and temporary (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Chrysostom): the glory of the old covenant was real but transient, destined to pass away — a mark of its provisional character, soon to be eclipsed by the abiding glory of the gospel.

themes: the ministration of death, written and engraven in stones, was glorious
the children of Israel could not stedfastly behold the face of Moses
the glory of the ministration of death
real but fading glory

schools.summary: Paul's a fortiori: if the ministration of death (the law) had glory, 'how shall not the ministration of the spirit (the gospel) be rather (much more) glorious?' The gospel is 'the ministration of the Spirit' — the dispensation through which the Holy Spirit is given and life imparted — over against the law, which gave death; its glory must therefore far exceed (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: since the gospel gives life by the Spirit, while the law gave death, its glory is incomparably greater.

themes: How shall not the ministration of the spirit be rather glorious
the surpassing glory of the ministration of the Spirit
the gospel gives life by the Spirit
the greater glory of the new covenant

schools.summary: Paul restates the contrast: the law is 'the ministration of condemnation' (it condemns the sinner), the gospel 'the ministration of righteousness' (it reveals and bestows the righteousness of God by faith); if the former had glory, the latter 'much more exceeds' — the gospel imparting justification through Christ, far surpassing the condemning law in glory (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Wesley: condemnation belongs to the law, righteousness to the gospel; the dispensation that justifies must be far more glorious than the one that condemns.

themes: if the ministration of condemnation be glory, much more doth the ministration of righteousness exceed in glory
the ministration of righteousness exceeds in glory
the justifying gospel surpasses the condemning law
greater glory of the gospel

schools.summary: That which 'was made glorious' (the law, with its real glory) now 'had no glory in this respect' — its glory is so eclipsed by the surpassing glory of the gospel that, by comparison, it has none; as the stars are unseen when the sun rises, so the glory of the law is lost in the far greater glory of the gospel (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: the comparison is relative — the law's glory was real, but so far surpassed by the gospel's that it seems as nothing in its presence.

themes: that which was made glorious had no glory in this respect
by reason of the glory that excelleth
the law's glory eclipsed by the gospel
stars before the rising sun

schools.summary: Paul concludes the comparison: 'that which is done away' (the law, the temporary dispensation, now abolished) was glorious; 'much more' must 'that which remaineth' (the gospel, the permanent, abiding covenant) be glorious; the gospel being the dispensation that 'remains,' enduring to the end, its abiding nature adding to its glory (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: the transitory had glory; the permanent must have far more; the gospel's perpetuity crowns its surpassing glory.

themes: if that which is done away was glorious, much more that which remaineth is glorious
the passing law and the abiding gospel
the gospel's perpetuity crowns its glory
transitory and permanent

schools.summary: 'Seeing we have such hope' — such a glorious, abiding ministry and its hope — Paul and the apostles 'use great plainness (boldness, openness) of speech,' preaching the gospel clearly and confidently, without concealment; the word meaning boldness, freedom, openness, unlike the veiled obscurity of the old covenant (Barnes, Henry).
Calvin: the clarity of the gospel ministry stands over against the veiled, obscure ministry of Moses; the glory of the new covenant warrants and produces this boldness.

themes: seeing then that we have such hope, we use great plainness of speech
Paul's boldness grounded in his hope
open, clear preaching of the gospel
unlike the veiled old covenant

schools.summary: Paul refers to Moses veiling his face (Exodus 34:33-35) after speaking with God, because his face shone — but gives it a symbolic turn: the veil prevented Israel from gazing on the 'end' (the termination, fading) of 'that which is abolished' (the transient glory of the law), symbolizing the obscurity of the old covenant, which hid from Israel that its glory was passing and pointed beyond itself to Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Moses's veiled ministry, obscuring the fading glory, stands over against the apostles' open, plain preaching; the old covenant was deliberately obscure, the new is clear.

themes: Moses, which put a vail over his face
that the children of Israel could not stedfastly look to the end of that which is abolished
the veil of Moses hiding the fading glory
the obscurity of the old covenant

schools.summary: Israel's 'minds were blinded' (hardened, dulled) — they did not perceive the true meaning and end of the law, a judicial and self-incurred blindness; the veil remains 'to this day' when the Jews read the old covenant (the Scriptures), still over their understanding, so they read but do not perceive that it points to Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the veil is taken away only 'in Christ' — when one comes to Christ (or, the veil is abolished by Christ); he is the key that unlocks the Old Testament's meaning.

themes: their minds were blinded
the same vail untaken away in the reading of the old testament; which vail is done away in Christ
Israel's blindness and the veil
the veil removed only in Christ

schools.summary: Paul repeats and intensifies: 'even unto this day,' when 'Moses is read' (the law, in the synagogue), 'the vail is upon their heart' — the obscurity is now located not on the text but on their own hearts; the veil has shifted from Moses's face to the Jews' own hearts, an inward, spiritual blindness that prevents them from seeing Christ in the Scriptures, the fault lying not in the Scripture but in the hardened reader (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: even unto this day, when Moses is read, the vail is upon their heart
the veil upon the heart of Israel
inward spiritual blindness
the fault in the hardened reader

schools.summary: The gracious promise (echoing Exodus 34:34, where Moses removed the veil when he turned to the Lord): when the heart 'turns to the Lord' (to Christ, in conversion), the veil is removed; turning to Christ takes away the veil of blindness, so the convert sees clearly the meaning of the Scriptures and the glory of the gospel (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): this holds out hope even for blinded Israel — whenever any Jew (or any blinded soul) turns to Christ, the veil is removed and the light breaks in.

themes: when it shall turn to the Lord, the vail shall be taken away
the veil removed by turning to the Lord
conversion lifting the blindness
hope for blinded Israel

schools.summary: Barnes: 'the Lord' is Christ, who is 'the Spirit' — the sum, substance, end, and life of the Old Testament (the 'spirit' of v.6), so that contemplated as centering in him the Scriptures become clear; and 'where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty' — freedom of speech, freedom from the dark Jewish prejudices and the bondage of sin, the liberty of the children of God. Robertson takes 'the Lord' as the Jehovah of Exodus 34:34 (the passage quoted), cautioning against pressing the identification of Christ and the Spirit; Ellicott: Christ's essential being is Spirit (John 4:24).

Calvin: this speaks not of Christ's essence but his office — Christ is the life-giving spirit of the law (the soul that quickens it, the 'spirit' of v.6), and the 'Spirit' in the next clause is the Holy Spirit he confers; he warns against the Arian misuse that would press 'the Lord is the Spirit' to deny Christ's true deity. Liberty is emancipation from the bondage of sin and the confidence of adoption (Romans 8:15).
Cyril: the Holy Spirit is living, speaking, sanctifying, with authority — the deity and personhood of the Spirit, who gives the liberty of the new covenant.

debates.question: What does 'the Lord is that Spirit' mean?

sides.position: Christ is the Spirit — the substance, end, and life of the Old Testament (implying his deity)
Christ as the life-giving spirit of the law in respect of office, not essence; the Spirit he confers — against the Arian misuse
'The Lord' is the Jehovah of Exodus 34:34 (the passage quoted); do not press the identification of Christ and the Spirit

themes: Now the Lord is that Spirit
where the Spirit of the Lord is, there is liberty
the Lord identified with the Spirit
the liberty of the Spirit

schools.summary: 'We all' (all believers, not Moses only) with 'open (unveiled) face' — no veil between us and God — behold 'as in a glass (mirror)' the glory of the Lord reflected in the gospel; and beholding it, 'are changed (transformed, metamorphosed) into the same image,' progressively conformed to Christ's likeness, 'from glory to glory' (from one degree of glory to another, ever-increasing, Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Cyril): this transformation is wrought 'by the Spirit of the Lord' — the Holy Spirit effects the progressive sanctification, conforming the believer to Christ's image from glory to glory.

themes: we all, with open face beholding as in a glass the glory of the Lord
are changed into the same image from glory to glory
the transforming vision of the Lord's glory
sanctification by the Spirit
